content: 2026-04-21 — Sam (31), backend/ML at a Paris logistics startup. Quick thinking-out-loud: we have 3M shipment embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, dim=384) in our RDS Postgres with pgvector. Daily ingest ~100k new vectors. Current infra: AWS RDS, 16 vCPU, 64 GB RAM, 1 TB storage, pgvector installed. Question: vector DB hype vs plain Postgres — when is pgvector enough? Constraints: budget ≈ €2k/month, p95 SLAs target <100 ms (ideally <50 ms) for top-k, expected growth to ~20M vectors by Oct 2026, QPS today ~200 reads, peak expected ~500 QPS. We need filtering: customer_id (~200k unique), status (4 values), pickup/delivery date ranges; joins back to orders table for price/region; multi-tenant isolation; retention 9 months (~27M vectors at peak). Ops time: team of 2 engineers, prefer low weekly ops. Thoughts out loud and options.
Do you prioritize latency or operational simplicity/cost more if you must choose one?

Both matter, so rank them: 1) latency (p95 target <50 ms), 2) total cost ≤ €2k/mo, 3) low ops (≤10 hours/week), 4) accurate recall (recall@10 ≥0.9). Empirical notes: I ran pgvector with HNSW on 3M — single-node RDS p95 ≈ 30–70 ms for k=10 with no filters. When I add metadata filters (customer_id or date range), latency often doubles because ANN candidate set and DB filtering interplay is inefficient. I haven't tested large-scale incremental inserts: adding 100k/day could make index maintenance painful. Backups: snapshotting whole DB is fine but index rebuilds worry me. I need migration options if pgvector hits limits.
Understood. How complex/selective are the typical filters in a query (high-cardinality customer_id vs broad date ranges vs combinations)?

Typical query patterns: 60% are customer-scoped (customer_id high-cardinality ~200k), often with a narrow date range (7–30 days). 30% are global searches with status filter (few values). 10% are ad-hoc cross-customer analytics. Most queries require joining shipment vectors with orders table to fetch price/region before final scoring. Multi-tenant isolation is required: queries must never leak other customers' vectors. So we need either per-tenant filtering or physically separated datasets.

So hybrid search (ANN candidate generation + precise DB filtering/joins) is a hard requirement. Would you accept a two-stage pipeline (ANN service returns ~K candidates, then Postgres filters/joins) or do you need single-system native filtering?

I can accept two-stage if it meets latency and recall. Implementation plan I'm leaning to: 1) baseline tests on pgvector (HNSW + tuned parameters) at scale to 20–27M vectors on a beefier node by 2026-05-01; 2) measure p95, recall@10, index build time, incremental insert impact, CPU/RAM, and backup times; 3) thresholds: if p95 >80 ms or ops >10 h/week or index rebuild >4 h, trigger migration. Migration targets: Milvus (self-hosted) or Qdrant for ops balance, or Pinecone if we pay for managed simplicity. Timeline: start tests 2026-05-01, evaluate 2026-08-01. Questions: what exact metrics to collect and what candidate sizes to benchmark?

Collect: p50/p95/p99 latency (k=1,5,10), recall@1/5/10 against brute-force baseline, candidate set sizes (N candidates returned), index build time and incremental insert throughput, CPU/RAM/disk I/O, filter-selectivity effects, and ops time. For candidates, do you want suggested K values to test?

Yes — test candidates K ∈ {100, 500, 1000}. Benchmarks: 1) no-filter vector-only queries; 2) high-selectivity filters (customer_id + date range); 3) low-selectivity filters (status only); 4) join+filter end-to-end latency including fetching order data. Run each scenario with synthetic query load for QPS ∈ {50, 200, 500} and measure recall vs brute-force on a 1000-sample query set. Acceptance criteria: recall@10 ≥0.9 for chosen K, p95 ≤50 ms at 200 QPS, p95 ≤80 ms at 500 QPS. If pgvector meets these, keep it for 6 months and reassess. Anything else to add?

Add: measure index memory residency (does HNSW fit RAM), cold vs warm cache latencies, and multi-tenant fault isolation (no cross-tenant leakage). Also capture cost projections for scaling to 20–27M on RDS vs a managed vector DB. Ready to draft the exact test plan with query scripts and monitoring dashboards for 2026-05-01?
